questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a circle have?
questions.hint: Any diameter is a line of symmetry for a circle.
questions.explanation: A circle has infinitely many lines of symmetry — every diameter is a line of symmetry. Option C would be correct for a square, not a circle.

source_trace.content_anchor: circle has infinite lines of symmetry

explanations_tiered.definition: A line of symmetry divides a figure into two identical mirror halves.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A circle is perfectly symmetric about any line through its center (any diameter). Therefore it has infinitely many lines of symmetry.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can fold a circle in half along any straight line through its middle — infinite ways to fold it perfectly in half.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Every diameter of a circle is a line of symmetry. Since there are infinitely many diameters, a circle has infinitely many lines of symmetry.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The circle is invariant under reflection across any line through its center. The set of such lines is infinite (uncountable), hence infinite lines of symmetry.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: line of symmetry = fold line that matches both halves.
Any line through center is a diameter.
Folding along any diameter maps circle onto itself.
misconceptions: Thinking a circle has only 4 lines (confusing with square).
Thinking a circle has no lines because it's round.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Circle: infinite lines of symmetry (every diameter).

glossary_entry.term: Lines of symmetry of a circle
glossary_entry.definition: A circle has infinitely many lines of symmetry — all diameters.

flashcard.front: How many lines of symmetry does a circle have?

visual_system.visual_title: Lines of symmetry of a circle
visual_system.visual_description: Circle with several diameters shown as dashed lines.
visual_system.caption: Every diameter is a line of symmetry.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle with multiple diameters as dashed lines, showing infinite symmetry.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How many lines of symmetry in a circle?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A circle has infinite lines of symmetry.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Every diameter is a line of symmetry.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a circle, points \(P\) and \(Q\) are symmetric with respect to line \(AB\). What must be true about line \(AB\) relative to segment \(PQ\)?

options.B: It is the perpendicular bisector of \(PQ\)
options.C: It is equal in length to \(PQ\)
options.D: It passes through the midpoint of \(PQ\) but is not perpendicular

questions.hint: Symmetry line is the perpendicular bisector of segment joining corresponding points.
questions.explanation: If P and Q are corresponding points for a line of symmetry AB, then AB is the perpendicular bisector of PQ. Option D is incomplete — perpendicular is required.

source_trace.content_anchor: if AB is line of symmetry, AB is perpendicular bisector of PQ

explanations_tiered.definition: If two points are mirror images across a line, that line is the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining them.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Line AB is the perpendicular bisector of PQ, meaning AB ⟂ PQ and AB passes through the midpoint of PQ.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you fold along AB, P lands on Q. The fold line AB cuts PQ exactly in half and at a right angle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reflection property: AB ⟂ PQ and AB bisects PQ (midpoint lies on AB). Thus AB is the perpendicular bisector of PQ.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For any reflection across line L, L is the perpendicular bisector of every segment joining a point to its image.
reasoning_steps: Identify P and Q as mirror images across AB.
Note that AB is the fold line.
Conclusion: AB ⟂ PQ and AB passes through midpoint → perpendicular bisector.
misconceptions: Thinking symmetry line is just a bisector without perpendicular.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Line of symmetry = perpendicular bisector of segment joining corresponding points.

glossary_entry.definition: The line of symmetry between two corresponding points is their perpendicular bisector.

flashcard.front: P and Q symmetric about line AB. What is AB to PQ?

visual_system.visual_description: Line AB vertical, points P left and Q right symmetric, AB perpendicular bisector of PQ.
visual_system.caption: AB ⟂ PQ and passes through midpoint.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Line AB, points P and Q symmetric, AB perpendicular bisector.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: P and Q symmetric about line AB. What is AB relative to PQ?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The line of symmetry is the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining corresponding points.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. AB is perpendicular bisector of PQ.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a circle, central angle \(AOB = 150^\circ\) and inscribed angle \(APB\) intercepts the same arc \(AB\). What is \(\text{m}(\angle APB)\)?
questions.hint: Inscribed angle = half of central angle subtending same arc.
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.6: \(m(\angle APB) = \frac{1}{2} m(\angle AOB) = \frac{1}{2} \times 150^\circ = 75^\circ\). Option A confuses inscribed with central angle.

explanations_tiered.definition: An inscribed angle is half the measure of its intercepted central angle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Given central angle 150°, inscribed angle = 75° by Theorem 5.6.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the big angle at the center is 150°, the smaller angle sitting on the edge watching the same arc is half: 75°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Direct application of Theorem 5.6: inscribed = ½ central.
reasoning_steps: Recall inscribed angle = half central angle (same arc).

formula_analysis.name: Inscribed angle theorem
formula_analysis.derivation: From isosceles triangle and exterior angle.

explanations_tiered.revision_note: Inscribed angle = ½ central angle (same arc).

glossary_entry.definition: m∠APB = ½ m∠AOB for same arc AB.

flashcard.front: Central angle 150°, same arc inscribed angle?

visual_system.caption: Inscribed angle = 75°.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle with central angle 150°, inscribed angle 75° same arc.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Central angle 150°, find inscribed angle same arc.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle with vertex on circle, half the central angle subtending same arc.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply angle properties of circles to solve problems

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is a cyclic quadrilateral?

options.A: A quadrilateral with all vertices on a circle
options.C: A quadrilateral with parallel opposite sides
options.D: A quadrilateral with one right angle

questions.hint: Cyclic = vertices lie on a circle.
questions.explanation: Definition 5.1: A cyclic quadrilateral has all four vertices lying on a circle. Option B describes a rhombus, not specifically cyclic.

source_trace.content_anchor: quadrilateral with a circle passing through all four vertices

explanations_tiered.definition: A cyclic quadrilateral is a quadrilateral whose four vertices lie on a single circle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: All vertices are concyclic (on the same circle). Not all quadrilaterals are cyclic; only those with supplementary opposite angles.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw a four-sided shape with all four corners touching the same circle — that's a cyclic quadrilateral.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Definition 5.1: There exists a circle passing through all four vertices W, X, Y, Z.
explanations_tiered.advanced: A quadrilateral is cyclic iff the sum of opposite angles equals 180° (Theorem 5.9).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: all vertices on same circle.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cyclic quadrilateral: all vertices on a circle.

glossary_entry.definition: A quadrilateral inscribed in a circle.

flashcard.back: All four vertices lie on a circle.

visual_system.caption: All vertices on the circle.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle with quadrilateral inside, all four corners touching circle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A quadrilateral with all vertices on a circle.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A quadrilateral whose vertices lie on a common circle.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Write angle properties of circles in own words
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. All four vertices lie on a circle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In circle with diameter \(AB\), point \(C\) lies on the circle. If \(\text{m}(\angle ABC) = 55^\circ\), what is \(\text{m}(\angle BAC)\)?
questions.hint: Angle in semicircle = 90°, then sum of angles in triangle = 180°.
questions.explanation: Thales' theorem: ∠ACB = 90°. Triangle sum: 55° + 90° + ∠BAC = 180° → ∠BAC = 35°. Option B is the right angle itself, not the angle sought.

explanations_tiered.definition: Thales' theorem: angle inscribed in a semicircle is 90°.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Given diameter AB, ∠ACB = 90°. Then in triangle ABC, ∠BAC = 180° − 90° − 55° = 35°.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If one triangle corner is on the circle and the opposite side is the diameter, that corner is 90°. Then find the missing angle.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By Thales, ∠ACB is right angle. Then complementary angles in right triangle: 55° + ∠BAC = 90° → ∠BAC = 35°.
misconceptions: Forgetting triangle sum after finding right angle.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Angle in semicircle = 90°. Then triangle sum.

flashcard.front: Diameter AB, C on circle, ∠ABC=55°. Find ∠BAC.

visual_system.visual_description: Circle with diameter AB, point C on circle, triangle ABC.
visual_system.caption: ∠BAC = 35°.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle diameter AB, C on circle, angle at B 55°, at C 90°, at A 35°.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Diameter AB, C on circle, ∠ABC=55°. Find ∠BAC.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle in semicircle is 90°, then triangle sum gives third angle.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply angle properties of circles to solve problems

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In cyclic quadrilateral \(ABCD\), \(\text{m}(\angle ABC) = 108^\circ\). Find \(\text{m}(\angle ADC)\).
questions.hint: Opposite angles in cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°.
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.9: Opposite angles supplementary → ∠ABC + ∠ADC = 180° → 108° + ∠ADC = 180° → ∠ADC = 72°. Option B would make them equal, not supplementary.

explanations_tiered.definition: In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles are supplementary.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In a four-sided shape inside a circle, angles across from each other add up to 180°. So 108° needs a partner of 72°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By Theorem 5.9: m∠ABC + m∠ADC = 180°. Substituting gives 72°.
worked_solution: Opposite angles in cyclic quad are supplementary.

formula_analysis.name: Cyclic quadrilateral opposite angles
formula_analysis.derivation: From inscribed angles intercepting arcs summing to 360°.

misconceptions: Thinking opposite angles are equal (true for parallelograms, not general cyclic quads).

visual_system.caption: ∠ADC = 72°.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle with quadrilateral ABCD, angle ABC 108°, opposite angle ADC 72°.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Quadrilateral with vertices on circle, opposite angles supplementary.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply angle properties of circles to solve problems

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors
questions.question: A circle has radius \(r\). What is the circumference?
questions.explanation: Circumference formula is \(C = 2\pi r\). Option B is area of circle, not circumference.

source_trace.section_anchor: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Circumference is the distance around a circle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: C = 2πr, where r is radius. π ≈ 3.14159.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk around the edge of a circle, that distance is 2 × π × radius.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived from π = C/d, so C = πd = 2πr.

visual_system.visual_description: Circle with radius r, circumference marked.
visual_system.caption: C = 2πr.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle radius r, circumference distance around.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is circumference of circle radius r?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors
questions.question: A segment is bounded by a chord of length \(10\,\mathrm{cm}\) and an arc of length \(15\,\mathrm{cm}\). What is the perimeter of the segment?
questions.hint: Perimeter of segment = chord length + arc length.
questions.explanation: Perimeter of a segment = chord length + arc length = \(10 + 15 = 25\,\mathrm{cm}\). Option C multiplies instead of adding.

source_trace.section_anchor: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Segment perimeter = straight chord length + curved arc length.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add chord (10 cm) and arc (15 cm) to get 25 cm.
explanations_tiered.beginner: To walk around a slice, you walk the straight line (chord) plus the curved edge (arc).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Perimeter of segment = chord length + arc length, no other components.

formula_analysis.name: Segment perimeter formula
formula_analysis.derivation: Boundary consists of chord and arc.

misconceptions: Forgetting chord is part of perimeter.

glossary_entry.definition: Sum of chord length and arc length bounding the segment.

flashcard.front: Segment: chord 10 cm, arc 15 cm. Perimeter?

visual_system.visual_description: Segment with chord 10 cm, arc 15 cm.
visual_system.caption: Perimeter = 25 cm.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle segment, chord 10 cm, arc 15 cm, perimeter 25 cm.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Segment: chord 10 cm, arc 15 cm. Perimeter?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Theorems on Angles and Arcs Determined by Lines Intersecting inside, on and outside a Circle
questions.question: A secant and a tangent intersect outside a circle, intercepting a major arc of \(200^\circ\) and a minor arc of \(40^\circ\). Find the angle between them.
questions.hint: Secant-tangent angle = ½ (major arc − minor arc).
questions.explanation: Angle = ½(200° − 40°) = ½(160°) = 80°. Option D is the difference without halving.

source_trace.section_anchor: Theorems on Angles and Arcs Determined by Lines Intersecting inside, on and outside a Circle

explanations_tiered.definition: Angle formed by a secant and a tangent outside a circle is half the difference of the intercepted arcs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Major arc 200°, minor arc 40°. Angle = ½(200 − 40) = 80°.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The angle outside is half the difference between the big arc and small arc.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By Theorem 5.11 (secant-tangent case): x = ½(major − minor).
reasoning_steps: Find major and minor intercepted arcs.

formula_analysis.name: Secant-tangent angle theorem
formula_analysis.derivation: From inscribed angles and exterior angle theorem.

glossary_entry.definition: Angle outside circle = half difference of arcs.

flashcard.front: Secant & tangent outside, arcs 200° and 40°. Find angle.

visual_system.visual_description: Circle, secant and tangent from external point, arcs 200° and 40°.
visual_system.caption: Angle = 80°.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle with secant and tangent, arcs 200° and 40°, angle 80°.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Secant & tangent, arcs 200° and 40°. Find angle.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply theorems on angles and arcs determined by lines intersecting circles

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors
questions.question: A chord subtends a central angle of \(90^\circ\) in a circle of radius \(8\,\mathrm{cm}\). Find the chord length.
questions.explanation: Chord length = \(2 \times 8 \times \sin(45^\circ) = 16 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 8\sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{cm}\). Option A would be the radius, not chord.

source_trace.section_anchor: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Chord length = 2 × radius × sin(half central angle).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw a right isosceles triangle inside circle: chord = radius × √2 times 2? Actually 8√2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: When θ = 90°, chord forms legs of right isosceles triangle with hypotenuse? Actually chord = √(r²+r²) = √(2r²) = r√2? Wait: chord = 2r sin45° = 2r(√2/2) = r√2. Here r=8 → 8√2 cm.

formula_analysis.name: Chord length formula
formula_analysis.derivation: From right triangle with opposite side c/2, hypotenuse r.

misconceptions: Using θ instead of θ/2 inside sine.

flashcard.front: Circle radius 8 cm, central angle 90°. Chord length?

visual_system.visual_description: Circle radius 8 cm, central angle 90°, chord length 8√2 cm.
visual_system.caption: Chord = 8√2 cm.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle radius 8 cm, central angle 90°, chord 11.31 cm.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Radius 8 cm, central angle 90°. Find chord.
